---
title: "Module 5 Assignment: Machine Learning on Scale"
author:
  - name: Holly Morehouse
    affiliations:
      - id: bu
        name: Boston University
        city: Boston
        state: MA
number-sections: true
date: '2024-11-21'
format:
  html:
    theme: cerulean
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
  docx:
    theme: cerulean
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
date-modified: today
date-format: long
execute:
  echo: false
  eval: true
  freeze: auto
---


## System Information {.unnumbered}

```{python}
# | eval: true
# | echo: true
import platform
import psutil

print("Operating System:", platform.system(), platform.release())
print("Python Version:", platform.python_version())
print("Machine:", platform.machine())
print("Processor:", platform.processor())

mem = psutil.virtual_memory()
print("Total Memory (MB):", round(mem.total / (1024**2), 2))
print("Available Memory (MB):", round(mem.available / (1024**2), 2))
```


## Start your assignment here



## Github Link
https://github.com/hjmorehouse89/A04-SP26-HJMorehouse89


```{python}
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession
from pyspark.sql import functions as F
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, when, pow, trim
from pyspark.ml import Pipeline
from pyspark.ml.feature import StringIndexer, OneHotEncoder, VectorAssembler
from pyspark.ml.regression import LinearRegression, RandomForestRegressor, GeneralizedLinearRegression
from pyspark.ml.evaluation import RegressionEvaluator

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import math
import os


spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("LightcastModule5").getOrCreate()
df = spark.read.option("header", "true") \
    .option("inferSchema", "true") \
    .option("multiLine", "true") \
    .option("escape", "\"") \
    .csv("./data/lightcast_job_postings.csv")

print("Loaded data")
#df.printSchema()
#df.show(5)


continuous_features = [
    "MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE",
    "MAX_YEARS_EXPERIENCE",
    "DURATION",
    "SALARY_FROM",
    "SALARY_TO",
]

categorical_features = [
    "EDUCATION_LEVELS_NAME",
    "REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"
]


target = "SALARY"
selected_cols = continuous_features + categorical_features + [target]
df_clean = df.select(*selected_cols)



df_clean.groupBy("MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE")\
           .agg(F.count("*").alias("count")) \
           .orderBy(F.col("MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE").desc())\
           .show(truncate=False)

df_clean = df_clean.withColumn("REMOTE_TYPE",
when(trim(col("REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"))== "Remote", "Remote")
.when(trim(col("REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"))== "Hybrid Remote", "Hybrid")
.when(trim(col("REMOTE_TYPE_NAME"))== "Not Remote", "Onsite")
.when(col("REMOTE_TYPE_NAME").isNull(),"Onsite")
.otherwise("Onsite"))

df_clean.select("REMOTE_TYPE_NAME").distinct().show(truncate=False)
df_clean.drop("REMOTE_TYPE_NAME")


df_clean = df_clean.withColumn("EXPERIENCE_GROUP",
when(col("MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE").between(0,1),"Internship/Entry Level")
.when(col("MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE").isNull(),"Junior")
.when(col("MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE").between(1,3),"Junior")
.when(col("MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE").between(3,5),"Mid-Level")
.when(col("MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE").between(5,10),"Senior")
.otherwise("Expert")
)

df_clean.show(5,truncate=False)

df_clean = df_clean.withColumn(
    "SALARY_CLEAN",
    F.when((F.col("SALARY").isNull()) | (F.col("SALARY") == 0) | F.isnan("SALARY"), None).otherwise(F.col("SALARY"))
).withColumn(
    "SALARY_FROM_CLEAN",
    F.when((F.col("SALARY_FROM").isNull()) | (F.col("SALARY_FROM") == 0) | F.isnan("SALARY_FROM"), None).otherwise(F.col("SALARY_FROM"))
).withColumn(
    "SALARY_TO_CLEAN",
    F.when((F.col("SALARY_TO").isNull()) | (F.col("SALARY_TO") == 0) | F.isnan("SALARY_TO"), None).otherwise(F.col("SALARY_TO"))
)
df_clean = df_clean.withColumn(
    "SALARY_FINAL",
    F.when(F.col("SALARY_CLEAN").isNotNull(),F.col("SALARY_CLEAN"))
    .when(
        F.col("SALARY_FROM_CLEAN").isNotNull() & F.col("SALARY_TO_CLEAN").isNotNull(),(F.col("SALARY_FROM_CLEAN") + F.col("SALARY_TO_CLEAN"))/2
    )
    .when(
        F.col("SALARY_FROM_CLEAN").isNotNull() & F.col("SALARY_TO_CLEAN").isNull(), F.col("SALARY_FROM_CLEAN")
    )
    .when(
        F.col("SALARY_TO_CLEAN").isNotNull() & F.col("SALARY_FROM_CLEAN").isNull(), F.col("SALARY_TO_CLEAN")
    )
    .otherwise(None)
)


for c in selected_cols:
    df_clean = df_clean.filter(col(c).isNotNull())

print("Cleaned row count:", df_clean.count())
df_clean = df_clean.withColumn(
    "MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE_SQ",
    pow(col("MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE"), 2)
)

indexers = [
    StringIndexer(inputCol=c, outputCol=f"{c}_idx", handleInvalid="keep")
    for c in categorical_features
]

encoders = [
    OneHotEncoder(inputCol=f"{c}_idx", outputCol=f"{c}_vec")
    for c in categorical_features
]

features_input = continuous_features + [f"{c}_vec" for c in categorical_features]

assembler = VectorAssembler(
    inputCols=features_input,
    outputCol="features"
)

poly_input = [
    "MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE",
    "MIN_YEARS_EXPERIENCE_SQ",
    "MAX_YEARS_EXPERIENCE",
    "DURATION",
    "SALARY_FROM"
] + [f"{c}_vec" for c in categorical_features]

poly_assembler = VectorAssembler(
    inputCols=poly_input,
    outputCol="features_poly"
)

pipeline = Pipeline(stages=indexers + encoders + [assembler, poly_assembler])
pipeline_model = pipeline.fit(df_clean)
final_df = pipeline_model.transform(df_clean)

final_df.select("SALARY", "features", "features_poly").show(5, truncate=False)


train_df, test_df = final_df.randomSplit([0.8, 0.2], seed=42)

print("Train count:", train_df.count())
print("Test count:", test_df.count())


lr = LinearRegression(
    featuresCol="features",
    labelCol="SALARY"
)

lr_model = lr.fit(train_df)
lr_predictions = lr_model.transform(test_df)


poly_lr = LinearRegression(
    featuresCol="features_poly",
    labelCol="SALARY"
)

poly_model = poly_lr.fit(train_df)
poly_predictions = poly_model.transform(test_df)


rf = RandomForestRegressor(
    featuresCol="features",
    labelCol="SALARY",
    numTrees=200,
    maxDepth=8,
    seed=42
)

rf_model = rf.fit(train_df)
rf_predictions = rf_model.transform(test_df)


rmse_eval = RegressionEvaluator(labelCol="SALARY", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="rmse")
r2_eval = RegressionEvaluator(labelCol="SALARY", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="r2")
mae_eval = RegressionEvaluator(labelCol="SALARY", predictionCol="prediction", metricName="mae")

def evaluate_model(name, predictions, model_summary=None, num_params=None):
    rmse = rmse_eval.evaluate(predictions)
    r2 = r2_eval.evaluate(predictions)
    mae = mae_eval.evaluate(predictions)

    aic = None
    bic = None

    if model_summary is not None:
        try:
            aic = model_summary.aic
        except:
            aic = None

        try:
            n = model_summary.numInstances
            deviance = model_summary.deviance
            dispersion = model_summary.dispersion
            k = num_params if num_params is not None else len(model_summary.coefficients) + 1

            log_likelihood = -0.5 * (
                n * math.log(2 * math.pi)
                + n * math.log(dispersion)
                + deviance / dispersion
            )
            bic = k * math.log(n) - 2 * log_likelihood
        except:
            bic = None

    return {
        "Model": name,
        "RMSE": rmse,
        "R2": r2,
        "MAE": mae,
        "AIC": aic,
        "BIC": bic
    }

lr_results = evaluate_model(
    "Linear Regression",
    lr_predictions,
    lr_model.summary,
    len(lr_model.coefficients) + 1
)

poly_results = evaluate_model(
    "Polynomial Regression",
    poly_predictions,
    poly_model.summary,
    len(poly_model.coefficients) + 1
)

rf_results = evaluate_model(
    "Random Forest",
    rf_predictions,
    None,
    None
)

results_df = pd.DataFrame([lr_results, poly_results, rf_results])
print(results_df)

```

## In the linear model based on the available data, we can approach it in the understanding that there is a straight correlation between salary and years of relevant experience. In this situation, a search may yield results that align with the belief that each added year of experience will result in an increased salary figure.

```{python}

# Linear model feature names
linear_feature_names = final_df.schema["features"].metadata["ml_attr"]["attrs"]
linear_names = []

for attr_type in linear_feature_names:
    for item in linear_feature_names[attr_type]:
        linear_names.append(item["name"])

coef_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Feature": linear_names,
    "Coefficient": list(lr_model.coefficients)
})

# Polynomial model feature names
poly_feature_names = final_df.schema["features_poly"].metadata["ml_attr"]["attrs"]
poly_names = []

for attr_type in poly_feature_names:
    for item in poly_feature_names[attr_type]:
        poly_names.append(item["name"])

poly_coef_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Feature": poly_names,
    "Coefficient": list(poly_model.coefficients)
})

print("\nPolynomial Regression Coefficients:")
print(poly_coef_df)

```

## In the polynominal regression, we can see a relationship of how salary and experience can curve in different ways. For instance, someone with a smaller amount of experience may see an increase in salary growth over time, but salary will slow down as experience goes on.

```{python}

lr_pd = lr_predictions.select("SALARY", "prediction").toPandas()
lr_pd["Model"] = "Linear Regression"

poly_pd = poly_predictions.select("SALARY", "prediction").toPandas()
poly_pd["Model"] = "Polynomial Regression"

rf_pd = rf_predictions.select("SALARY", "prediction").toPandas()
rf_pd["Model"] = "Random Forest"

all_preds = pd.concat([lr_pd, poly_pd, rf_pd], ignore_index=True)
all_preds.to_csv("output/model_predictions.csv", index=False)


fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 10))
axes = axes.flatten()

plot_data = [
    ("Linear Regression", lr_pd),
    ("Polynomial Regression", poly_pd),
    ("Random Forest", rf_pd)
]

for i, (name, data) in enumerate(plot_data):
    axes[i].scatter(data["SALARY"], data["prediction"], alpha=0.5)
    min_val = min(data["SALARY"].min(), data["prediction"].min())
    max_val = max(data["SALARY"].max(), data["prediction"].max())
    axes[i].plot([min_val, max_val], [min_val, max_val], linestyle="--")
    axes[i].set_title(name)
    axes[i].set_xlabel("Actual SALARY")
    axes[i].set_ylabel("Predicted SALARY")

axes[3].axis("off")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("output/model_comparison.png")
plt.show()
plt.close()


rf_feature_names = linear_names
rf_importances = rf_model.featureImportances.toArray()

print("RF feature names:", len(rf_feature_names))
print("RF importances:", len(rf_importances))
 
```

## The random forest regression is meant to help interpret based on data in a scenario based situation, with the guide of various decision trees. In this context, it might be related to looking up a salary range, based on factors like years experience and relevant work experience.

```{python}

rf_imp_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Feature": rf_feature_names,
    "Importance": rf_importances
}).sort_values("Importance", ascending=False).head(10)

rf_imp_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Feature": rf_feature_names,
    "Importance": rf_importances
}).sort_values("Importance", ascending=False).head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.barplot(data=rf_imp_df, x="Importance", y="Feature")
plt.title("Top 10 Feature Importances - Random Forest")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("output/rf_feature_importance.png")
plt.show()
plt.close()


results_df.to_csv("output/model_metrics.csv", index=False)
coef_df.to_csv("output/linear_coefficients.csv", index=False)
poly_coef_df.to_csv("output/polynomial_coefficients.csv", index=False)

print("Done. Files saved in output/")
spark.stop
```
